source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
2
  Gregory Shaﬀer, ‘Transnational Legal Process and State Change’ (2012) 37 Law & Social Inquiry 229,
  233–4.
3
  For example, the case of Germany: Three per cent of the German GDP are linked to the Chinese market.
  The average German ﬁrm raises seven per cent of its income in China, for DAX corporations it is 15 per
  cent, one-third of Inﬁneon’s revenues comes from China, for the car industry it is 20 per cent—and the
  German federal state of Niedersachsen holds 20 per cent of the shares of VW. Inﬁneon is not planning to
  decrease this dependency but hopes for an increase in sales in China due to the increase in electric cars,
  which need more chips. Handelsblatt, ‘German ﬁrms facing perfect storm as Chinese growth slackens’ (18
  January 2019) www.handelsblatt.com/today/companies/red-scare-german-ﬁrms-facing-perfect-storm-
  as-chinese-growth-slackens-/23884056.html?ticket=ST-7819176-vK9D9ssabe5HdGJSQqTI-ap2.
4
  Christoph Giesen and Max Hägler, Urumqi/München, ‘Der Druck auf deutsche Unternehmen steigt’ Süd-
  deutsche Zeitung (9 June 2021) www.sueddeutsche.de/wirtschaft/china-basf-volkswagen-industrie-eu-
  uiguren-1.5316206 and Christoph Giesen, ‘Chinademonstriert seine Macht’ Süddeutsche Zeitung (14
  June 2021) www.sueddeutsche.de/wirtschaft/china-sanktionen-menschenrechte-1.5320972.
5
  Nico Krisch, ‘International Law in Times of Hegemony: Unequal Power and the Shaping of the Inter-
  national Legal Order’ (2005) 16(3) The European Journal of International Law 369.
6
  Ibid.
7
  See Shaﬀer (n 2) 253.
8
  Claire Cutler, ‘Legal Pluralism as the “Common Sense” of Transnational Capitalism’ (2013) 3(4) Oñati
  Socio-Legal Series 719.
9
  Possibly with the Cyber Belt and Road Initiative. Fan Liang, Vishnupriya Das, Nadiya Kostyuk and
  Muzammil M Hussain, ‘Constructing a Data-Driven Society: China’s Social Credit System as a State Sur-
  veillance Infrastructure’ (2018) 10(4) Policy & Internet 415, 435.
10
   Before 2014 the concept of a social credit system was discussed but was exclusively focused on
   ﬁnancial data. See Liang and others (n 9) 424. The main document describing the envisioned
   nation-wide system combining ﬁnancial and social aspects is the ‘Planning Outline for the Construc-
   tion of a Social Credit System (2014–2020)’, online: https://chinacopyrightandmedia.wordpress.com/
   2014/06/14/planning-outline-for-the-construction-of-a-social-credit-system-2014-2020/, the original
   document: www.gov.cn/zhengce/content/2014-06/27/content_8913.htm In section 2, the outline
   for regulating corporate conduct can be found.
 296         S. JAKOB
